text:
CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Mr. Raymond Okafor is a 58-year-old male with known stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D mutant, MSS) with bilobar hepatic metastases and bilateral pulmonary metastases. He has undergone prior sigmoid colectomy and has been receiving second-line systemic chemotherapy with FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (irinotecan at reduced dose 150 mg/m2 plus 5-fluorouracil/leucovorin plus ramucirumab 8 mg/kg every 14 days) for approximately 6 months, now completing 12 total cycles. This study is obtained for routine restaging assessment. Prior imaging reference: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis from approximately 8 weeks prior showed a partial response to second-line therapy (hepatic target lesion sum 4.2 cm, pulmonary nodules measuring 7 mm RLL, 5 mm LUL, 5 mm RML). CEA most recently 49.8 ng/mL, mildly rising from 41.2 ng/mL. CA 19-9 118 U/mL.

TECHNIQUE:
Helical CT imaging of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed on a 64-slice multidetector CT scanner. IV contrast (omnipaque 350, 125 mL) was administered via peripheral IV at 3.5 mL/sec. Chest images acquired in the portal venous phase; abdomen and pelvis imaged in portal venous and 3-minute delayed phases for hepatic lesion characterization. Axial reconstructions at 5 mm thickness with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformats generated. Oral contrast administered approximately 45 minutes prior to imaging. No known contrast allergy. Creatinine 1.04 mg/dL (acceptable for IV contrast administration).

COMPARISON:
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast from approximately 8 weeks prior (cycle 4 restaging, second-line therapy) and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast from the time of documented disease progression (approximately 6 months prior, reflecting initiation of second-line therapy).

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The airways are patent to the level of the segmental bronchi bilaterally. Lung parenchyma is well-inflated without consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural effusion.

Pulmonary Nodules: The previously identified pulmonary nodules are again demonstrated and are essentially stable in size compared to the most recent prior examination:
- Right lower lobe: A well-circumscribed, smoothly marginated soft tissue nodule measuring 7 mm in greatest axial dimension (unchanged from 7 mm on prior CT). This nodule is located in the posterobasal segment, subpleural in position.
- Left upper lobe: A soft tissue nodule in the posterior segment of the left upper lobe measuring 6 mm (previously 5 mm on the most recent prior CT; note this nodule had measured 6 mm on the earlier comparison study at the time of progression detection). The slight measurement discrepancy between studies is within the limits of measurement variability and slice selection. Morphology is unchanged -- well-defined, non-calcified, rounded.
- Right middle lobe: A small nodule in the medial segment of the right middle lobe measuring 5 mm (stable). Smooth margins, no internal calcification.

No new pulmonary nodules are identified. No cavitation within any of the above nodules. No endobronchial lesions. No ground-glass halos or irregular spiculation to suggest superimposed infection.

Pleura: No pleural effusions bilaterally. No pleural thickening or nodularity to suggest pleural implants.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinal and hilar contours are within normal limits for patient age. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes (all visualized nodes measure under 1 cm in short-axis dimension). The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. The aorta and great vessels are unremarkable. Mild atherosclerotic calcification of the thoracic aorta, not hemodynamically significant.

Chest Wall and Osseous Structures: A right-sided subcutaneous implanted venous access port device is present with catheter tip appropriately positioned at the cavoatrial junction. No new osseous lesions in the visualized thoracic skeleton. No pathologic fractures.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with smooth hepatic contour. No capsular retraction or morphologic changes to suggest underlying cirrhosis. The previously characterized hepatic metastatic lesions are again demonstrated as hypodense foci on the precontrast series with variable peripheral enhancement on arterial phase and relative washout on delayed imaging, consistent with their known metastatic nature:

- Hepatic segment II: A hypodense lesion measuring 1.1 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 1.0 cm on the prior CT; originally 0.9 cm at cycle 6 restaging). Margins remain well-defined. Enhancement pattern unchanged. This lesion is very slightly larger than on the immediately preceding study, though the change is at the margin of measurement precision.

- Hepatic segment IVa: A hypodense lesion measuring 1.2 cm in greatest axial dimension (unchanged from 1.2 cm on prior CT). Margins are well-defined with subtle peripheral enhancement. Adjacent hepatic parenchyma is unremarkable.

- Hepatic segment VI: A hypodense lesion measuring 0.8 cm (slightly decreased from 1.0 cm on prior CT and from 1.1 cm at 6-month restaging). Morphology is stable. No new satellites in the vicinity.

- Hepatic segment VII: The dominant hepatic lesion in segment VII measures 1.9 cm in greatest axial dimension (slightly decreased from 2.0 cm on the prior CT, and substantially smaller than the 3.2 cm lesion noted at the time of initial diagnosis). The lesion remains round and well-circumscribed, abutting a right hepatic vein tributary. No internal necrosis or hemorrhage. No significant interval change in hepatic vein relationship.

No new hepatic lesions are identified. No new satellite foci in proximity to the known lesions. The intrahepatic biliary ductal system is not dilated. No perihepatic fluid.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: The gallbladder is present and contains a few small hyperdense foci consistent with gallstones; no gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, or ductal dilatation to suggest acute cholecystitis or biliary obstruction. The common bile duct measures 5 mm (within normal limits).

Pancreas: The pancreas is homogeneous in attenuation with normal lobulation and no ductal dilatation. No pancreatic masses, inflammatory change, or peripancreatic lymphadenopathy.

Spleen: The spleen is homogeneous and normal in size at approximately 11.4 cm craniocaudal dimension. No splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands: Both adrenal glands appear normal without discrete nodules or masses. No adrenal hyperplasia.

Kidneys and Ureters: Both kidneys enhance symmetrically and excrete contrast normally. No hydronephrosis or obstructing stones. A few bilateral simple cortical cysts (largest 1.3 cm, right kidney) are incidentally noted, unchanged. No renal masses.

Gastrointestinal Tract:
The stomach is unremarkable with normal wall thickness. The small bowel loops demonstrate uniform wall enhancement without dilatation, thickening, or evidence of obstruction.

Post-sigmoid colectomy changes are present. The colorectal anastomosis is identified in the left lower pelvis with intact staple line; the anastomotic wall is of normal thickness without luminal narrowing, perianastomotic mass, or abnormal mural enhancement to suggest locoregional recurrence. The colon proximal and distal to the anastomosis appears normal in caliber. No pericolonic stranding or soft tissue density surrounding the anastomosis.

No free air or free fluid in the abdomen. No bowel obstruction.

Peritoneum and Mesentery: No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity, omental cake, or mesenteric soft tissue masses to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. The mesenteric fat is clear. No significant mesenteric lymphadenopathy -- scattered small mesenteric nodes measuring up to 8 mm in short axis, which represent residual reactive nodes or post-treatment change, not convincingly pathologic by size criteria.

Vascular Structures: The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber without aneurysmal change. The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, inferior mesenteric artery, and their major branches are patent without evidence of arterial occlusion or thrombosis. The portal vein and hepatic veins are patent and of normal caliber. No portal vein thrombosis.

Abdominal Lymph Nodes: There are no new pathologically enlarged retroperitoneal or para-aortic lymph nodes. Scattered small lymph nodes are present in the porta hepatis and gastrohepatic ligament, the largest measuring 9 mm in short axis -- stable in size and morphology compared to prior examination.

PELVIS:

Urinary Bladder: The urinary bladder is incompletely distended with a small amount of excreted contrast, limiting evaluation of the bladder mucosa. No grossly thickened wall or obvious intraluminal mass is identified.

Prostate and Seminal Vesicles: The prostate gland is mildly enlarged, consistent with the patient's age (estimated volume approximately 35 cc by visual inspection). No discrete prostatic nodules. The seminal vesicles are symmetric.

Bony Pelvis and Axial Skeleton: No lytic or sclerotic lesions in the visualized pelvis, sacrum, or visualized lumbar spine. No new osseous lesions to suggest skeletal metastases. Mild degenerative change at the L4-L5 disc space.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes: No pathologically enlarged pelvic or inguinal lymph nodes.

Other: No pelvic free fluid. Post-operative changes from prior sigmoid colectomy are again noted. No pelvic collections or anastomotic complications.

IMPRESSION:

1. HEPATIC METASTATIC DISEASE -- ESSENTIALLY STABLE WITH MINIMAL FLUCTUATION. The four known hepatic lesions (segment II, IVa, VI, and VII) demonstrate no meaningful interval change when accounting for expected measurement variability between examinations. The segment VI lesion shows very subtle further decrease in size. The dominant segment VII lesion measures 1.9 cm, marginally smaller than on the prior study. Individually, these findings reflect ongoing disease control without convincing growth or reduction beyond measurement reproducibility. The overall hepatic metastatic burden appears largely unchanged from the previous assessment, consistent with plateau following prior partial response to second-line therapy.

2. PULMONARY NODULES -- STABLE. The three previously identified bilateral pulmonary metastases in the right lower lobe (7 mm), left upper lobe (6 mm), and right middle lobe (5 mm) are essentially unchanged compared to the most recent prior examination. No new pulmonary nodules are identified. The left upper lobe nodule shows 1 mm apparent increase, which is not considered clinically significant given the inherent variability of sub-centimeter nodule measurement.

3. NO EVIDENCE OF ANASTOMOTIC RECURRENCE. The sigmoid colectomy anastomotic site appears intact and without features of locoregional tumor recurrence.

4. NO NEW SITES OF METASTATIC DISEASE. No new hepatic, pulmonary, peritoneal, osseous, or distant nodal lesions identified.

5. No ascites or pleural effusion.

6. INCIDENTAL: Right-sided port-a-cath with tip at cavoatrial junction, intact and appropriately positioned. Small bilateral renal cortical cysts, benign and unchanged. Mild prostatic enlargement. Lumbar degenerative disc disease.

Overall, the imaging findings are consistent with stabilization of metastatic disease across both the hepatic and pulmonary compartments under ongoing second-line systemic therapy. The extent of disease is essentially unchanged compared to the prior examination approximately 8 weeks ago.
